summary.headline: Northfield Mfg Ltd weekly payroll with hardcoded tax bands, dual pension schemes, 35-year-old overtime rules, and a dead-but-load-bearing field, all frozen since a 2009 ‘temporary’ tax patch.

rules.title: EMP-NAME width change breaks PAYRPT04 and extract job
rules.plainEnglish: The EMP-NAME field length (28 bytes) is coupled to PAYRPT04 and a 1999 extract job; widening it without rebinding those programs will corrupt their data layout.
rules.riskWhy: Hidden coupling between copybook length and two external jobs risks silent corruption if width changes without coordinated rebind.

rules.title: Hire date stores YYMMDD with century pivot 66, bonus uses hardcoded 2026
rules.plainEnglish: Employee hire date is held as two-digit year; years >=66 are treated as 19YY, else 20YY. Service bonus is then computed as (2026 − hire year), making both the pivot and the current year hardcoded and liable to break after 2065 or if not recompiled annually.

constants.name: Pivot
constants.meaning: Century pivot for hire date windowing

constants.name: Current year
constants.meaning: Hardcoded reference year for service calculation

rules.riskWhy: Windowed year logic will misattribute centuries after 2065; the hardcoded current year requires a source change each calendar year.

rules.title: Dead EMP-LV-BAND field retained for PAYRPT04 offset
rules.plainEnglish: EMP-LV-BAND has been unused since 2008 but must remain in the copybook because PAYRPT04 still references its memory offset; removing it would silently shift other fields.
rules.riskWhy: Dead field is load-bearing for a downstream report; removal causes silent data misalignment without compiler error.

rules.title: Standard work week is 37.5 hours
rules.plainEnglish: The normal working week is 37.5 hours; weekday and Saturday hours in excess of this trigger overtime.

constants.name: WS-STD-WEEK
constants.meaning: Standard weekly hours per Works Council Agreement 1987

rules.riskWhy: Contractual baseline that rarely changes, but any change would require recompilation.

rules.title: Overtime capped at 20 hours per week
rules.plainEnglish: Overtime hours exceeding 20 in a week are discarded; any extra hours are paid at plain rate (no multiplier), as per Memo 02/117.

constants.name: WS-OT-CAP
constants.meaning: Maximum weekly overtime hours eligible for premium pay

rules.riskWhy: Cap was raised once and may need adjustment again; excess hours silently forfeit premium.

rules.title: Overtime multiplier is 1.50
rules.plainEnglish: Overtime hours are paid at time-and-a-half (1.50 times effective hourly rate).
rules.riskWhy: Long-standing rate; change requires recompile but no dynamic drift.

rules.title: Sunday rate is double time (2.00)
rules.plainEnglish: All Sunday hours are paid at double time (2.00 multiplier), regardless of total hours worked.

constants.name: WS-SUN-MULT
constants.meaning: Sunday premium multiplier per union agreement 96/2

rules.riskWhy: Rate is stable, but hardcoded.

rules.title: Night shift differential is a 12% rate uplift, not a lump sum
rules.plainEnglish: When both timecard night flag and employee night eligibility are 'Y', the base hourly rate is multiplied by 1.12; the uplift applies to all hours worked in that shift, not as a separate allowance.
rules.riskWhy: The rate and the uplift logic are hardcoded; a change to lump-sum would require rewrite.

rules.title: Union dues are a flat $4.20 per week
rules.plainEnglish: Employees flagged as union members have exactly $4.20 deducted from net pay each week (check-off).
rules.riskWhy: Flat rate may become stale; not linked to union schedule or CPI.

rules.title: Sick pay is a flat $23.35 per day, not updated here
rules.plainEnglish: Statutory sick pay is calculated at a fixed daily rate of $23.35; the program comment explicitly warns not to uprate this value in code—HR handles changes elsewhere.

constants.name: WS-SICK-DAILY
constants.meaning: Daily statutory sick pay rate (do not uprate here)

rules.riskWhy: Hardcoded rate is almost certainly stale; updating it requires external HR action, creating drift risk.

rules.title: Grade hourly rates are hardcoded, last uprated April 2019
rules.plainEnglish: Seven pay grades (G1–G7) have base hourly rates coded directly in the INIT table: 9.84, 10.75, 11.90, 13.20, 14.75, 17.10, 19.95. No dynamic lookup from a rate file.
rules.riskWhy: Rates are 2019 values and will not reflect current pay awards; every change requires a program source edit and recompile.

rules.title: PAYE tax bands are hardcoded since 2009 ‘temporary’ patch
rules.plainEnglish: Weekly PAYE uses: 0% on first £242.00, 20% on income between £242.01 and £967.00, 40% above £967.00. These thresholds and rates are frozen in source code.
rules.riskWhy: Hardcoded tax thresholds and rates have not tracked fiscal changes since 2009; employees may be systematically over- or under-taxed.

rules.title: NI uses same thresholds as tax, computed on gross pay
rules.plainEnglish: NI contributions: 8% on gross pay between £242.00 and £967.00, 2% above £967.00. The thresholds are identical to the tax bands; any change to one inadvertently changes the other.
rules.riskWhy: Coupling of NI and tax thresholds via shared constants means a fix in one always affects the other; stale hardcoded values compound the risk.

rules.title: Dual pension schemes with hardcoded contribution rates
rules.plainEnglish: Standard auto-enrolment: employee 5%, employer 3%. Employees in grades G6/G7 remain on a legacy scheme (closed 2004): employee 6.5%, employer 9%. An opt-out flag sets both contributions to zero regardless of grade.
rules.riskWhy: Rates are hardcoded and may have changed since 2013 (auto-enrolment) or 2004 (legacy); grandfathering logic ties grade to scheme, making scheme closure difficult.

rules.title: Service bonus: 10+ years = 2%, 25+ years = 3.5% of basic pay
rules.plainEnglish: Employees receive a bonus as a percentage of basic pay: 2% for 10–24 years' service, 3.5% for 25+ years. Service years are computed from the windowed hire date against hardcoded year 2026.
rules.riskWhy: Bonus calculation depends on hardcoded current year and windowing pivot; failures will occur after 2065 and if not manually recompiled each year.

rules.title: Pension EE contribution reduces taxable pay (net pay arrangement); computation order is fixed
rules.plainEnglish: Taxable income is calculated as gross minus employee pension contribution. The code must compute pension before tax; the program contains a warning not to reorder paragraphs 400-TAX and 550-PENSION.
rules.riskWhy: Paragraph reordering would silently corrupt taxable income calculation; comment emphasises a long-standing dependency requiring explicit knowledge.

rules.title: NI is computed on gross pay, ignoring pension—historic quirk
rules.plainEnglish: National Insurance contributions are always calculated on gross pay, not on pension-adjusted income. This is a deliberate anomaly kept for parity with the 1999 rewrite and re-signed by Finance at each audit.
rules.riskWhy: Deviation from fiscal norm is only maintained by institutional memory; removal or change could trigger audit failure and under/overpayment.

rules.title: Negative net pay is clamped to zero with exception
rules.plainEnglish: If net pay after all deductions becomes negative, it is set to £0.00 and an exception report is written.
rules.riskWhy: Guard prevents negative payments but masks deduction configuration errors.

rules.title: Sunday hours are excluded from the overtime threshold
rules.plainEnglish: Only weekday and Saturday hours count towards the 37.5-hour standard week; Sunday hours never trigger overtime and are always paid separately at the Sunday rate.
rules.riskWhy: Nuanced overtime rule documented in union agreement; hardcoded logic would be easy to misinterpret during maintenance.

rules.title: Pension opt-out flag overrides all contributions
rules.plainEnglish: If EMP-PEN-OPTOUT = 'Y', both employee and employer pension contributions are set to zero, irrespective of grade or legacy scheme entitlement.
rules.riskWhy: Opt-out bypasses scheme rules; if the flag is mis-sourced, employees may miss contributions with no alert.

rules.title: Do not change tax paragraph without contacting Pensions Desk X4471
rules.plainEnglish: Any modification to paragraph 400-TAX requires prior contact with the Pensions Desk at extension X4471.
rules.riskWhy: External dependency inscribed in code; tax logic changes affect pension contribution calculations and may breach regulatory arrangements.
